item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Officially one of my favorite SCPs. I love the enigmatic angle where it seems like all of it's claims come from a parallel dimension, or perhaps the sea creature just reality-bent it all up, cottage, collection, and housekeeper et al.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of my absolute favorite articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just want to say that this is one of my all-time favorite SCPs, and I love the Blackwood tales. I do wonder though, does he even notice that he's underwater? Do they feed him? Does he perceive everyone else as giants, or the same size as himself?
And why deny his request for reading material?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've just recently learned about the Count of Saint Germain, and I'm wondering if there was any inspiration there. Either way, another Smapti tale connecting the two would probably be wonderful, unless he's already been referenced in a Smapti tale and I just don't remember.

item.title: Mod Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Nudibranch_-_Nembrotha_kubaryana.jpg

Image Source, CC BY-SA 3.0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just realized our dear Lord Blackwood have his own tag. I love this skip, and I will see how much it was expanded.
Congratulations on a score of +666! It was very satisfying giving the last vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This makes me so happy and I don't know why. It just does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad to see this is so highly rated. It's fun to see SCPs like this that are a little more light-hearted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dammit, this needs to be canonized. Someone get on making B.T. Moth a POI.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
“Dr. B. T. Moth’s Effective Particle Destabilizers.”

Ah, yes, the inestimable B.T. Moth, inventor of Moth Helm. Boon to man and child… and moth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I LOLed at the end of Addendum-2.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is from quite a while ago, but upon re-reading it and the comments, I had something of a thought. So, SCP-319 was created by an associate and/or rival of Lord Blackwood in, presumably, the universe good ol' Woody is from.

What if it worked?

Sir Lawhead-Smythe's whole goal was to create a "door beyond our universe"; the fact that 319 is in the SCP's universe is an indication, I think, of the fact that it worked. What if Blackwood came to investigate after Lawhead-Smythe's presumable disappearance and was sucked into the same door that brought 319, which also transferred his consciousness into a sea slug. Given this hypothesis, this means that there is potentially another British-explorer-turned-sea-creature wandering around somewhere…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How did I not upvote this before?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was under the impression Lord Blackwood was just going to be a steam/diesel/clock/whatever-punk pastiche, and was directed here while going through the tales section. I am pleasantly surprised. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of my favourite SCP's, I can't help it but read Lord Blackwood's dialogue with the voice of Stephen Fry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Er, apologies for the necro — but there are several tales depicting Blackwood's adventures. :P You can check them out, they're tagged under "Blackwood". :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
UPVOTED, because I love the concept enough.

Really, if we could have tales depicting his adventures, it would make my day.

And I like to think that an older version of the Foundation was formed in the Victorian Era at the height of the British Empire, what with all the explorers and archaeologists and naturalists during that time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is ridiculous. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice, strange, and funny. Remides me of 2020. Upvote for you.
Also, I read 1867's lines in the voice of Godfrey from Fallout 3.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this one, interesting that it spawned numerous tales based on this one concept. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*invertebrate

And yeah, we do have a lot of non-human things that think they're human or act like their human. Point is, they're relatively different from eachother to merit their existence on the site as separate articles.

And we also have that plane that acts like a bird. I'm very fond of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's pretty much the original.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: WE HAVE SO MANY OF THESE. ITS AN ANIMAL WHAT THINKS ITS A HUMAN. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This will forever be my favourite SCP.

A british adventurer in the body of a sea slug and doesn't know it.

Brilliant!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He's from an alternate universe of awesome. I want to see a similar SCP declaring itself Theodore Roosevelt, and put it together with this one for much great benefits to mankind. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's not a very nice thing to say. I think you've hurt Djoric's feelings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey.
Hey Djoric.
I love you for this article.
Like- A lot.
*cough* Lord Blackwood is one of the things that made me want to cease lurking and join the site.
The perfect ammount of obsurdity and eerieness as to what sort of crazy shit he did and how he turned into a seaslug- Just- Yes.
All of the yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reminds me of Jeff Dunham.

[:|-ing intensifies]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be fair, name any other slug in existence who can, you know, answer you, with defiance or acknowledgement, it is or is not a slug. Blackwood has a point, is all I'm sayin'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reminds me of Jeff Dunham.

Jeff: So, Achmed, you know you're dead right?
Achmed: WHAT?

The sea slug totally doesn't believe it is a slug. That's fun.

item.title: Re: +300
item.content:encoded:
+ DANCE DANCE BANANA
- PARTY BEGIN NOW

why you so huge image?

item.title: Re: +300
item.content:encoded: PARTY BEGIN NOW.

oh joy,
we must celebrate!!!
(Why did I forget to upvote this earlier?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He kinda posted it at the top of this discussion page ten months ago, but thanks anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thought you might like to know that you were good enough to be turned into an audio recording by The Volgun.

http://m.youtube.com/watch?v=vygK6iZBAdU

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What about a D-class? Hell, I'd volunteer to be Blackwood's page-turner. I'd even read the books to him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The sea slug thing doesn't really add anything to this other than randomness (and I hate randomness).

I guess it could be personal taste, but I found the sea slug to be whimsical, not just random. I thought it was fitting that such an esteemed and accomplished gentleman would be, well, a slug and not aware of it. (Also I find the mental picture of a sea slug oozing contentedly in a small dish of Earl Grey tea to be adorable.)

That said, happy belated birthday, Lord Blackwood. Tea and crumpets on me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
:|

Look, if "sea slug that thinks/is a British gentleman explorer with a massive collection of weird shit in his basement" isn't a hook, I honestly don't know what is. It'd be boring if he was human. It would be blase, trite, overdone. Predictable. The sea slug serves a very potent purpose: it is the hook. It is the thing that you remember. A human British gentleman explorer with a collection of oddities, bah, forgettable. A sea slug? Comes completely out of left field, runs you over in a dented Ford pickup, and you remember it.

Consider the Lightbulb Fucker from John Dies at the End. It's a man-o-war jellyfish that floats around inside a house and tries to have sex with lightbulbs. No explanation, no plot importance, it simply is. Is it random? yes. Came from nowhere with no explanation. Does it detract from the story in any way? No, because by this point John Dies at the End has established time and time again that weird, unexplainable batshit loony fuckery happens all over the place. (The spider monsters wear wigs attached with rubber bands, the Chinese restaurant still has the mural from the Mexican place it replaced and is run by brothers from the Czech republic, Ronald McDonald ate his own intestines, badgercondas exist)

I operate with the belief that the Foundation universe is split 50/50 absurdity/horror. Weird, unexplainable batshit loony fuckery is how the Foundation-verse works.

e: unrelated, Blackwood is now a year and three days old

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The sea slug thing doesn't really add anything to this other than randomness (and I hate randomness). There's some good pieces of content here, but it needs a better hook.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My own feeling is that Lord Blackwood may have been anomalous long before he became a sea slug. He was able to adventure to places no one else could, places that may or may not exist outside their interaction with Lord Blackwood. And, once he got home, he found many people perfectly incredulous, despite all the evidence he had collected. Unfortunately, sometime around 1910, he ran into something that slugified him.

item.title: Re: based on...
item.content:encoded: Ah, but it is important to acknowledge which tales you have read, for they paint a different picture indeed, as the majority were not written by Blackwood's author (who is me.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i personally imagine him as a green garden slug
i know it says sea slug though
in his stories i imagine him as sir hammerlock
posted that without reading jorics

item.title: based on...
item.content:encoded:
based on his stories, i believe he is from an alternate dimension, and somehow in crossing into ours, he and a few that followed were turned into slugs
and deeds' anomalous properties theoretically are cross dimensional

its less farfetched than the alternative that blackwood is a liar. that is impossible

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Or maybe he was the only one who remained a sea slug…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy doritoes…
I don't know what the hell happened to this guy. I'd go with an "alternate reality transfer" theory, except for the fact that not only he, but his housekeeper and his entire collection teleported WITH him, and HE was the only one who became a sea slug.
A +1 from me.

item.title: Re: I made an audio version of this !
item.content:encoded: Fantastic as usual, Volgun.

item.title: I made an audio version of this !
item.content:encoded: http://youtu.be/eHnwug3aYck - This was fun. I hope i did it justice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sir Hammerlock's very title screen reads "Hunter. Scholar. Gentleman." I think it fits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I always figured he sounded like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Professor Elemental

YESSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSS

+11 respect points, my good man!

Though I don't see Blackwood looking like that, personally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I always pictured him speaking like as the father from Murder At Moorstone Manor from Ripping Yarns.

"He was the only man I ever knew who was squeezed to death by an anaconda."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just a pair of silly ideas.

What Blackwood might look like.

What Blackwood might sound like.

e: Or this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Finally working through the notorious Blackwood stories— and I have to say, I'm a sucker for this whole concept. My single favorite literary character is Dr. Stephen Maturin, a British naturalist who features in the Aubrey-Maturin novels. Seeing the gentleman explorer trope wrapped up in SCP conceits and paranormal mindfuckery is positively glorious.

terminology and style appropriate to late nineteenth century England

Might be a bit tongue-in-cheek (A CUP OF TEA, MY GOOD MAN, WHAT WHAT), but strikes just the right tone. +200.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
His name is "Lord" Blackwood. He's turned into a sea slug, presumably after he dies. I think that he regenerated from "human" to sea-slug after death.

For you see, Lord Blackwood is really…

TIME Lord Blackwood!

…please kill me.

item.title: Re: NAMES
item.content:encoded: Bumping?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But Deeds recognized him. Not just complied, but recognized him in at least one of your works on him. I'm counting the "Ask Lord Blackwood" as a joke tale. A good one of course, but still.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this so much, and it definitely gets a +1 from me, but…

If he communicates telepathically, did the conversation require a stenographer of some kind? I guess it doesn't really matter, but that's the kinda stupid detail I pick up on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I couldn't not upvote after that last line. XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's a theory I haven't heard about Lord Blackwood yet:

His consciousness was transferred into the Sea Slug in a similar matter to how SCP-963 works.
